metadata.summary: Pre-commit to cloud capacity via Reserved Instances or Savings Plans for 1-3 years to achieve 60-75% discounts on predictable steady-state workloads.

content.title: Reserved Instances and Savings Plans
content.summary: Commitment-based pricing (Reserved Instances and Savings Plans) delivers 60-75% discounts over on-demand for predictable steady-state workloads. The key discipline is to rightsize before committing, target 70-80% of baseline compute, and start conservative with Convertible RIs to preserve flexibility while the commitment strategy matures.

why.p: On-demand pricing is a premium for the privilege of not committing. When a workload runs continuously for months or years, the cloud provider will always charge the highest possible rate without a commitment. Reserved Instances and Savings Plans transfer risk to the buyer in exchange for a significant discount — typically 60-75% depending on term length and payment option.

The trap is over-commitment: buying 3-year Standard RIs for 100% of capacity, then having usage drop due to efficiency improvements or architectural changes. Starting at 70-80% of minimum observed usage and using Convertible RIs for flexibility eliminates most of this risk.

list.item: Production databases, application servers, and analytics clusters that run 24/7 without interruption.
Workloads with at least 6 months of stable usage history to establish a reliable baseline.
Any environment where on-demand compute exceeds $5,000/month — the break-even on a 1-year RI is typically 7-9 months.
After rightsizing: always rightsize first, then commit to the correct size.
Variable or bursty workloads with no stable baseline — commit only to the floor, use on-demand or Spot for burst.
Workloads scheduled to be decommissioned or migrated within the commitment period.
Environments where the architecture is actively evolving (consider shorter 1-year terms or Convertible RIs instead).

section.name: ri-types
section.p: Reserved Instance comparison by type and savings potential:

list.item: Standard RI (3-year): up to 75% savings, locked to instance family and size
Standard RI (1-year): up to 60% savings, locked to instance family and size
Convertible RI: 60-68% savings, allows exchange to different instance family, OS, or tenancy

rule.statement: Start RI purchases with Convertible RIs at 70-80% of minimum observed on-demand usage. Use Standard RIs only for workloads with 12+ months of stable history and no planned architecture changes.
rule.rationale: Convertible RIs can be exchanged; Standard RIs cannot be changed without selling on the RI Marketplace (which may not always be possible). Starting at 70-80% of minimum usage means even if load drops 20%, the commitment is still fully utilized.

section.name: savings-plans
section.p: AWS Savings Plans offer commitment flexibility beyond traditional RIs:

list.item: Compute Savings Plans: apply to any EC2 instance, Lambda, and Fargate usage — most flexible
EC2 Instance Savings Plans: apply to specific instance families in a region — slightly higher discount than Compute SPs
Commitment unit: dollars per hour (e.g., $10/hr), not instance count
Recommended approach: model 80% of minimum hourly spend, commit to that amount

section.name: hybrid-strategy
section.p: Optimal cloud efficiency comes from blending pricing models. The recommended hybrid baseline:

example.title: Hybrid instance pricing strategy
example.code:
Workload distribution for optimal efficiency: Baseline (70%) → Reserved Instances / Savings Plans Predictable burst (20%) → On-Demand Scale-out / batch (10%) → Spot Instances
Real case (E-Commerce Platform, $150,000/month on-demand): Baseline (60%): 3-year Standard RIs → $36,000/month (60% off on-demand) Growth buffer (20%): 1-year Cvt RIs → $15,000/month (50% off on-demand) Seasonal burst (15%): On-Demand → $22,500/month Batch (5%): Spot → $750/month (90% off on-demand) Total: $74,250/month (50.5% overall savings)
example.note: Layer pricing models for maximum efficiency. Reserve flexibility for unknown workloads.

example.title: 100% on-demand with no commitments
example.code: # 100% on-demand, $150,000/month # No commitment-based pricing # No seasonal adjustment # Result: paying maximum price for stable baseline load # Annual overspend vs optimal hybrid: ~$900,000
example.note: On-demand is appropriate for burst and variable workloads only. Stable baseline should always be covered by commitments.

list.item: Identify steady-state workloads (running 720+ hours/month consistently)
Review historical usage patterns for 6+ months (seasonal awareness)
Calculate break-even point: at what month does RI cost = equivalent on-demand?
Model coverage scenarios: what percentage of baseline to cover at what term?
Start conservative: commit to 70-80% of minimum usage, not average or peak
Schedule quarterly RI review: utilization monitoring, expiry management, resizing

rule.statement: Target 70-80% RI/SP coverage of baseline compute. Monitor RI utilization monthly and alert if any RI drops below 80% utilization — this signals waste or a workload change that needs addressing.
rule.rationale: Under 70% coverage leaves significant on-demand spend that should be committed. Over 80% without a detailed analysis risks over-committing. RI utilization below 80% means the commitment is generating waste rather than savings.

list.item: Monitor RI utilization daily via Cost Explorer or equivalent
Use RI Marketplace to sell unused Standard RIs if workloads change
Exchange Convertible RIs proactively when instance family migration is planned
Track expiry dates and budget for renewals 60+ days in advance
Review Savings Plans recommendations in Cost Optimizer monthly

antipattern.description: Purchasing 3-year Standard RIs for 100% of current capacity without accounting for workload evolution.
antipattern.reason: Workloads shrink due to rightsizing, efficiency improvements, or architectural changes. Over-committed Standard RIs cannot be easily exited; losses can total tens of thousands per month for large environments.

antipattern.description: Committing before rightsizing — purchasing RIs for oversized instances.
antipattern.reason: You lock in the waste. Rightsize first, validate performance, then commit to the correct smaller instance. Committing to m5.2xlarge when m5.large is sufficient means paying 3-year committed prices for twice the capacity you need.

antipattern.description: Treating RI coverage as a one-time purchase decision reviewed annually.
antipattern.reason: Cloud environments change constantly. Quarterly RI reviews catch misalignments before they become expensive. An RI purchased 18 months ago for a service that was rightsized 6 months ago is wasting money daily.

section.name: prompts
section.prompt:
Based on this usage data, recommend a Reserved Instance strategy:
Current on-demand spend: $[amount]/month Usage pattern (last 6 months): [Paste instance usage data showing hours/month by instance type]
Constraints: - Budget for upfront: $[amount] - Risk tolerance: [conservative/moderate/aggressive] - Provider: [AWS/GCP/Azure] - Planning horizon: [1 year/3 years]
Please provide: 1. Recommended RI purchases by instance type and term 2. Standard vs Convertible RI split with rationale 3. Payment option recommendation (all upfront/partial/no upfront) 4. Expected savings calculation 5. Break-even analysis 6. Coverage percentage achieved 7. Residual on-demand for flexibility

Analyze this Reserved Instance portfolio for optimization:
Current RIs: [List: Instance type, Quantity, Term remaining, Utilization %]
Current on-demand usage: [List: Instance type, Average hours/month]
Please identify: 1. Underutilized RIs that should be sold or exchanged 2. Unused capacity that could be reallocated 3. On-demand usage that should be covered by new RIs 4. Expiring RIs and renewal recommendations 5. RI Marketplace selling opportunities

checklist.check: Rightsize all target instances before purchasing any RIs
Collect 6+ months of usage history to identify seasonal patterns
Model RI vs Savings Plan coverage scenarios with break-even analysis
Start with Convertible RIs for flexibility during initial commitment period
Target 70-80% coverage of baseline compute (not average, not peak)
Set up RI utilization monitoring with alerts below 80% utilization
Schedule quarterly RI review in calendar
Track RI expiry dates 60+ days in advance for renewal planning
Evaluate Compute Savings Plans for Fargate/Lambda workloads
